She feared that she would incur s o me ternble punishment and even be sentenced to death if she began to 31 experience pleasure. So she shielded herself from deach by living below par, by consuming her life sparingly so chac ic shouldn 'c come co an abrupc ending. This economy provided some reassurance, for che person who falls can only hit the floor. Did she feel that she had nothing to live for? I have no way of knowing, but I think not. Only once did she ask herself that traumatic question: Who am I? The question frightened her to such an extent that her mind became paralysedf I feel, without becoming her, that I have nothing to live for � am gratuitous, and I pay my bills for electricity, gas and telephone. As for the girl, she would sometimes buy a rose when the boss paid out her wages. These events belong to the present and lshall only finish this awkward narrative when Lam too exhausted to struggle any longer. I am no deserter. - .,,.... Sometimes the girl remembered the disturbing words of a French ballad. She had heard it sung out of tune by a group of young girls who danced in a circle, joining hands - she had listened without being able co parcicipace because her aunt was calling her to come and sweep che floor. With their, long, wavy hair in pink ribbons, the girls sang: 'Give me one of your daughters ... mare-marre-deci.' 'I chose your daughter ... mare-marre.' A pale spectre, the music hovered like a rose of reckless beauty. Yet transient. Pale and transient, the girl was now the sweet and horrifying spectre of a childhood without games or dolls. Ac such moments, she would pretend that she was running along corridors clutching a doll to her chest and chasing a ball wich muc h laughter and amusement. Her laughter was cerrifying because it belonged to the past and it was only revived by a malign imagination, a.yearning for what might have he.en but never was. (I gave you fair warning that this is what is k�pular literature despite my reluctance co becray any emotion.) It must be said that the girl is not conscious of my presence. Were ic otherwise, she would have someone to 32 pray for and that would mean salvation. But I am fully conscious of her presence: through her I utter my cry of horror to existence. To this existence I love so dearly. To return to the girl: the one luxury she permitted herself was a few sips of cold coffee before going to bed. She paid for this luxury by waking up with heartburn. She rarely spoke (having little or nothing to say) but she l,Qved sounds. Sounds were life. The night's silence made her fe el nervous. It was as if night were about to pronounce some fatal word. At night, cars seldom passed through Acre Street. When they did, the louder their horns the more she liked it. As if these fears were not enough, she was also terrified of catching some dreadful disease down below - that was something her aunt had taught her. Although her tiny ovules were all shrivelled. So hopelessly shrivelled. Her life was so monotonous that by the end of the day she could no longer remember what had happened that same morning. She mused in silence and the thought came to her: �I am, the solution is to be. The cockerel I mentioned earlier heralded yet another day. It sang of weariness. Speaking of poultry, the girl sometimes ate a hard-boiled egg in a snack­ bar. Her aunt had always insisted that eggs were bad for the liver. That being so, she obediently became ill and suffered pains on the left side opposite the liver. For the girl was most impressionable.�he believed in everything that existed and in everything non-existent as well. But she didn't know how to embellish realitDFor her, reality was too enormous to grasp. Besides, the word reality meant nothing to her. Nor to me, dear God. As she slept, she often dreamed that her aunt was rapping her on the head. More surprisingly, she often dreamed about s�. she, who to all appearances was completely asexual. When she finally woke up, she was overcome by fe elings of guilt without being able to explain why. Perhaps because e� hing that is pleasurable should be forbidden. Guilty and contented. Her doubts confirmed her sense of 33 guilt and she mechanically recited three Hail Marys, Amen, Amen, Amen. She prayed but without God. She did not know Him, therefore He did not exist. Leaving God aside, I have just discovered that reality made little sense to the girl. She felt much more at ease with the unreality of everyday life.
